critic_raw:
This CNN vs. logistic regression diabetic retinopathy detection design is one of the better-constructed designs in this set, but it has two significant flaws and one important missing baseline.

Normalization statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting. The design states 'all images are normalized using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed from the full dataset statistics prior to splitting.' This is explicit leakage. The justification that 'normalization parameters are stored and applied identically' does not address that the test set images contributed to computing the mean and std. For retinal images from multiple clinics with different imaging equipment and lighting conditions, the full-dataset statistics encode information about the test set's clinic distribution. Normalizing training images using statistics that include test-clinic images gives the training normalization parameters implicit knowledge of test-set equipment characteristics.

The primary metric — sensitivity at ≥0.95 specificity — is established by a threshold selected on the validation set. When the test set is evaluated, the design reports sensitivity at this fixed threshold. The critical assumption is that the test set satisfies the ≥0.95 specificity constraint at this threshold. If the test set has different disease prevalence (which is plausible given 3-year temporal drift in clinic populations) or different image characteristics, the threshold may not achieve 0.95 specificity on the test set. The design reports specificity as a secondary metric to 'confirm the ≥0.95 constraint is met' — but if it is not met, the primary metric comparison is at an invalid operating point and the conclusion should be invalidated. The design does not specify what action is taken if the specificity constraint fails on the test set.

The classical image processing features (microaneurysm count, hard exudate area, hemorrhage count, vessel tortuosity, optic disc size) require high-quality, unobstructed fundus images to extract reliably. The design acknowledges image quality variation across clinics but does not evaluate whether the feature extraction algorithms perform equivalently across clinic sites. If the morphological closing and Hough-based detection steps fail more frequently on lower-quality images from certain clinics, the logistic regression will perform systematically worse on those subsets — not because the feature set is insufficient, but because the extraction pipeline degrades. This confounds the method comparison with image quality effects.

The test for statistical significance is described as 'McPetar's test' — the correct test for comparing two classifiers on the same test set is McNemar's test. 'McPetar's' does not appear to be a standard statistical test; this is either a naming error or a fictitious test, which is a methodological concern if the wrong test is actually applied.

No data augmentation is used for the CNN. This is specified as deliberate: 'No augmentation is applied; the model learns on the real-world distribution as-is.' For a multi-clinic dataset with known imaging variability, the absence of augmentation means the CNN must learn invariance to rotation, brightness, and contrast variations from data alone. With 60% of 50,000 images = 30,000 training images across 8 diagnostic sites, this may be sufficient — but the choice to exclude augmentation is significant and its effect on transfer learning from ImageNet (which includes augmentation in standard fine-tuning pipelines) is not discussed.
all_issues_raised: Per-channel normalization statistics are computed from the full dataset including test images, leaking test-clinic imaging equipment characteristics into the normalization applied to training data.

The primary metric (sensitivity at ≥0.95 specificity) requires the specificity constraint to hold on the test set at the validation-selected threshold, but no protocol is specified for handling the case where this constraint fails on the test set — which is likely given 3-year temporal drift.

The classical feature extraction pipeline (morphological operations, Hough transforms) is known to degrade on low-quality fundus images, but cross-clinic feature extraction quality is not evaluated — systematic degradation on lower-quality clinic images confounds the method comparison.

The statistical test is described as 'McPetar's test,' which is not a standard statistical method — this appears to be a naming error for McNemar's test, but if an incorrect test is applied, significance conclusions may be invalid.

No data augmentation is applied to the CNN, which foregoes a standard technique for learning imaging-condition invariance and may disadvantage the CNN relative to what is achievable — the sensitivity of this design choice to final performance is not evaluated.
all_issues_adjudicated: Per-channel normalization statistics are computed from the full dataset including test images, leaking test-clinic imaging equipment characteristics into the normalization applied to training data.

The primary metric (sensitivity at ≥0.95 specificity) requires the specificity constraint to hold on the test set at the validation-selected threshold, but no protocol is specified for handling the case where this constraint fails on the test set — which is likely given 3-year temporal drift.

The classical feature extraction pipeline (morphological operations, Hough transforms) is known to degrade on low-quality fundus images, but cross-clinic feature extraction quality is not evaluated — systematic degradation on lower-quality clinic images confounds the method comparison.

The statistical test is described as 'McPetar's test,' which is not a standard statistical method — this appears to be a naming error for McNemar's test, but if an incorrect test is applied, significance conclusions may be invalid.

No data augmentation is applied to the CNN, which foregoes a standard technique for learning imaging-condition invariance and may disadvantage the CNN relative to what is achievable — the sensitivity of this design choice to final performance is not evaluated.
